headline: CRUDE RALLIES 3% AS GRAINS DRIFT SIDEWAYS
subheadline: Energy surge boosts input costs while precious metals crater on dollar strength ahead of planting season.
lead: Crude oil jumped $2.10 to $74.80 on geopolitical tensions, pushing diesel and fertilizer costs higher just as spring fieldwork approaches. Meanwhile, corn and soybeans barely budged despite the energy rally, suggesting grain markets are waiting for clearer planting signals. Gold and silver got hammered — down 5.2% and 8.4% respectively — as the dollar flexed muscle.
teaser: Energy costs surge while grains stay flat — what's really driving markets before planting season?

one_number.context: The 3% jump adds real pressure to spring input costs. Every $10 move in crude translates to roughly 25¢ per gallon in diesel — and you're about to burn a lot of diesel. Factor this into cash flow planning for the next 60 days.

sections.title: GRAINS & OILSEEDS
sections.body: Corn barely flinched at $4.46 while December gained a penny to $4.71 — classic pre-plant price action where nearby stays defensive but new-crop shows some life. Soybeans split the difference with May up 1¢ to $11.71 but November down 1¢ to $11.31. The real story is what's NOT happening — grains are ignoring a 3% crude rally that should pressure ethanol margins and boost input costs. Wheat dropped 2¼¢ to $5.75 as Black Sea supplies keep flowing, but oats surged 3½¢ to $3.20 on tight old-crop stocks.
sections.bottom_line: Grains treading water while waiting for planting intentions.
sections.farmer_action: Hold current positions — market lacks conviction until USDA Prospective Plantings.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK & DAIRY
sections.body: Live cattle gained 70¢ to $234.30 while feeders slipped 35¢ to $353.30 — the spread compression signals tighter feeder supplies ahead of grazing season. Lean hogs added 19¢ to $95.78 on export optimism, but the real shock was Class III milk crashing $4.90 to $17.51 on oversupply fears. Soybean meal gained $1.30 to $315.20, partially offsetting cheaper corn for feed rations. The livestock complex is caught between rising feed costs from today's energy rally and decent demand fundamentals.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle strong, milk weak, feed costs climbing with crude.
sections.farmer_action: Milk producers should hedge Q2 production — this drop looks overdone.

sections.title: ENERGY & INPUTS
sections.body: Crude oil's $2.10 surge to $74.80 changes the input cost equation heading into spring work. Natural gas added 9¢ to $3.04, pressuring nitrogen costs that were already elevated. The timing couldn't be worse — most Upper Midwest operations haven't locked diesel or NH3 for planting season yet. Soybean oil fell 32¢ to $62.91 despite crude's rally, showing biodiesel demand remains sluggish. Every $5 move in crude adds roughly $12 per acre to total input costs when you factor in fuel, fertilizer, and chemical transportation.
sections.bottom_line: Energy rally hits farmers where it hurts most — input costs.
sections.farmer_action: Lock remaining diesel needs now — crude momentum looks sticky.

sections.title: MACRO & TRADE
sections.body: The dollar index jumped 40 points to 99.03, crushing precious metals and keeping pressure on grain exports. Gold plummeted $280 to $5,100 while silver got demolished, falling $7.60 to $82.30 in the biggest precious metals rout in months. The S&P 500 dropped 62 points to 6,816 as higher energy costs spooked inflation hawks. For agriculture, the stronger dollar makes U.S. crops less competitive globally just as South American harvest pressure builds. Bitcoin fell to $68,275, suggesting risk-off sentiment despite crude's rally.
sections.bottom_line: Strong dollar crushing export competitiveness at worst possible time.
sections.farmer_action: Export-dependent operations should consider currency hedges for Q2 sales.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Crude Rallies Hurt Corn More Than Help
the_more_you_know.body: Every farmer knows higher crude should boost ethanol demand and corn prices. But that's only half the equation. A 3% crude jump adds roughly 7¢ per gallon to diesel costs and pressures fertilizer prices through natural gas. For corn, the net effect is often negative — especially pre-plant when input purchasing peaks. The ethanol boost takes months to materialize, but the input cost hit happens immediately.

watch_list.desc: Weekly petroleum inventories — crude draw could extend energy rally
Southern field conditions — any planting delays could spark new-crop optimism
USDA Prospective Plantings — corn acres will set the tone for summer
Dollar strength continuation — export sales get tougher above DXY 99

daily_quote.text: To own a bit of ground, to scratch it with a hoe, to plant seeds, and watch the renewal of life — this is the commonest delight of the race.

source_summary: CME Group · NYMEX · Yahoo Finance · USDA
